From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Annual celebration held on 31 October

For the slasher multimedia franchise, see [*Halloween* (franchise)](/wiki/Halloween_(franchise) "Halloween (franchise)"). For other uses, see [Halloween (disambiguation)](/wiki/Halloween_(disambiguation) "Halloween (disambiguation)").

"All Hallows' Eve" redirects here. For other uses, see [All Hallows' Eve (disambiguation)](/wiki/All_Hallows%27_Eve_(disambiguation) "All Hallows' Eve (disambiguation)").

**Halloween**[[a]](#cite_note-9)[[b]](#cite_note-12) is a celebration [observed in many countries](/wiki/Geography_of_Halloween "Geography of Halloween") on 31 October, the eve of the [Western Christian](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") feast of [All Hallows' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day"). It is at the beginning of the observance of [Allhallowtide](/wiki/Allhallowtide "Allhallowtide"),[[11]](#cite_note-13) the time in the Christian [liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year") dedicated to remembering the dead, including [saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint") ([hallows](/wiki/Hallows "Hallows")), [martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyr "Christian martyr"), and all the faithful departed.[[3]](#cite_note-Surrey2014-3)[[12]](#cite_note-Mackinder2024-14)[[13]](#cite_note-15) In popular culture, Halloween has become a celebration of [horror](/wiki/Horror_fiction "Horror fiction") and is associated with the [macabre](/wiki/Macabre "Macabre") and the [supernatural](/wiki/Supernatural "Supernatural").[[12]](#cite_note-Mackinder2024-14)[[14]](#cite_note-rogers_m-16)

One theory holds that many Halloween traditions were influenced by [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") [harvest festivals](/wiki/Harvest_festival "Harvest festival"), particularly the [Gaelic](/wiki/Gaels "Gaels") festival [Samhain](/wiki/Samhain "Samhain"), which are believed to have [pagan](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism") roots.[[15]](#cite_note-Smith2004-17)[[16]](#cite_note-Christianity1-18)[[17]](#cite_note-Christianity2-19)[[18]](#cite_note-Christianity3-20) Some theories go further and suggest that Samhain may have been [Christianized](/wiki/Christianization "Christianization") as All Hallows' Day, along with its eve, by the [Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church").[[19]](#cite_note-Roberts1987-21)[[1]](#cite_note-Fasting-1)[[20]](#cite_note-22) Other academics say Halloween began independently as a [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") holiday, being the [vigil](/wiki/Vigil "Vigil") of All Hallows' Day.[[21]](#cite_note-FoleyO’Donnell2008-23)[[22]](#cite_note-Barr2016-24)[[23]](#cite_note-25)[[24]](#cite_note-26) Celebrated in [Ireland](/wiki/Ireland "Ireland") and [Scotland](/wiki/Scotland "Scotland") for centuries, [Irish](/wiki/Irish_people "Irish people") and [Scottish](/wiki/Scottish_diaspora "Scottish diaspora") immigrants brought many Halloween customs to North America in the 19th century,[[25]](#cite_note-rogers_nw-27)[[26]](#cite_note-Brunvand,_Jan_2006._p.749-28) and then through American influence various Halloween customs spread to other countries by the late 20th and early 21st centuries.[[14]](#cite_note-rogers_m-16)[[27]](#cite_note-Colavito,_Jason_2007._pp.151-29)

Popular activities during Halloween include [trick-or-treating](/wiki/Trick-or-treating "Trick-or-treating") (or the related [guising](/wiki/Guising "Guising") and [souling](/wiki/Souling "Souling")), attending [Halloween costume](/wiki/Halloween_costume "Halloween costume") parties, carving pumpkins or turnips into [jack-o'-lanterns](/wiki/Jack-o%27-lantern "Jack-o'-lantern"), lighting [bonfires](/wiki/Bonfire "Bonfire"), [apple bobbing](/wiki/Apple_bobbing "Apple bobbing"), [divination](/wiki/Fortune-telling "Fortune-telling") games, playing [pranks](/wiki/Practical_joke "Practical joke"), visiting [haunted attractions](/wiki/Haunted_attraction_(simulated) "Haunted attraction (simulated)"), telling frightening stories, and watching [horror or Halloween-themed films](/wiki/List_of_films_set_around_Halloween "List of films set around Halloween").[[28]](#cite_note-Fieldhouse2017p256-30) Some Christians practice the observances of All Hallows' Eve, including attending [church services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") and lighting [candles](/wiki/Votive_candle "Votive candle") on the graves of the dead,[[29]](#cite_note-Skog2008-31)[[30]](#cite_note-Duke2014-32)[[31]](#cite_note-33) although it is a secular celebration for others.[[32]](#cite_note-Hynes1993-34)[[33]](#cite_note-Kernan2013-35)[[34]](#cite_note-BradenVillage1988-36) Historically, some Christians [abstained from meat](/wiki/Meat-free_days "Meat-free days") on All Hallows' Eve, a tradition reflected in the eating of certain [vegetarian](/wiki/Christian_vegetarianism "Christian vegetarianism") foods on this day, including apples, [potato pancakes](/wiki/Potato_pancake "Potato pancake"), and [soul cakes](/wiki/Soul_cake "Soul cake").[[35]](#cite_note-37)[[36]](#cite_note-38)[[37]](#cite_note-Mahon1991-39)[[38]](#cite_note-Fieldhouse2017-40)

## Etymology

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/99/Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg/40px-Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Wiktionary-logo-en-v2.svg)

Look up ***[Halloween](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/Halloween "wiktionary:Halloween")*** in Wiktionary, the free dictionary.

*Hallow* derives from [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English") *halowen*, from [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") hālig meaning *[holy](/wiki/Holy "Holy")* and has been used synonymously with the word *[saint](/wiki/Saint "Saint")*.[[39]](#cite_note-41)[[40]](#cite_note-WilsonFischer2005-42)[[41]](#cite_note-DiehlDonnelly2001-43) The word *Halloween* or *Hallowe'en* comes from the [Lowland Scots](/wiki/Scots_language "Scots language") form of *All Hallows' Eve* (the evening before [All Hallows' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day")):[[42]](#cite_note-44) *even* is the Scots term for 'eve' or 'evening',[[43]](#cite_note-Contraction-45) and is contracted to *e'en* or *een*;[[44]](#cite_note-46) so *(All) Hallow(s) E(v)en* became *Halloween*. A term equivalent to 'All Hallows Eve' as attested in Old English.[[45]](#cite_note-oed-47) Thus, the name has an origin in [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"),[[46]](#cite_note-DSL-48)[[47]](#cite_note-49) and means '[Saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint")' eve(ning)'.[[48]](#cite_note-Luck1998-50)

## History

### Christian origins and historical customs

Halloween is influenced by Christian beliefs and practices surrounding [All Hallows' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day") (All Saints' Day).[[49]](#cite_note-Hopwood2019-51)[[22]](#cite_note-Barr2016-24)[[50]](#cite_note-Douris2024-52) The English word 'Halloween' comes from "All Hallows' Eve", being the evening before the Christian holy days of All Hallows' Day (All Saints' Day) on 1 November and [All Souls' Day](/wiki/All_Souls%27_Day "All Souls' Day") on 2 November.[[51]](#cite_note-rogers_religion-53) Since the time of the [early Church](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity"),[[52]](#cite_note-54) [major feasts](/wiki/Calendar_of_saints#Ranking_of_feast_days "Calendar of saints") in Christianity (such as [Christmas](/wiki/Christmas "Christmas"), [Easter](/wiki/Easter "Easter") and [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost")) had [vigils](/wiki/Vigil#Eves_of_religious_celebrations "Vigil") that began the night before, as did the feast of All Hallows.[[53]](#cite_note-Benham1887-55)[[49]](#cite_note-Hopwood2019-51) These three days are included in the liturgical period of [Allhallowtide](/wiki/Allhallowtide "Allhallowtide"), a time when [Western Christians](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") honour all [Christian martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyrs "Christian martyrs") and [saints](/wiki/Saint "Saint"), as well as [pray](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") for departed [souls](/wiki/Soul_in_the_Bible "Soul in the Bible").[[50]](#cite_note-Douris2024-52)

After the [persecution of Christians in the Roman Empire](/wiki/Persecution_of_Christians_in_the_Roman_Empire "Persecution of Christians in the Roman Empire"), "there were more [martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyr "Christian martyr") than there were days in the year, and so one day was set apart in honor of them all, and called All Saints' Day."[[54]](#cite_note-Mossier-56) Commemorations of all saints and [martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyr "Christian martyr") were held by several churches on various dates, mostly in springtime.[[55]](#cite_note-CP-57) In 4th-century Roman [Edessa](/wiki/Edessa "Edessa") it was held on 13 May, and on that date in 609, [Pope Boniface IV](/wiki/Pope_Boniface_IV "Pope Boniface IV") [re-dedicated](/wiki/Christianized_sites "Christianized sites") the [Pantheon](/wiki/Pantheon,_Rome "Pantheon, Rome") in Rome to "St Mary and all martyrs".[[56]](#cite_note-Saunders-58) This was the date of [Lemuria](/wiki/Lemuria_(festival) "Lemuria (festival)"), an [ancient Roman](/wiki/Religion_in_ancient_Rome "Religion in ancient Rome") festival of the dead, when it was believed that restless and vengeful souls wandered.[[57]](#cite_note-59) Some folklorists also suggest the ancient Roman festival of [Parentalia](/wiki/Parentalia "Parentalia") (including [Feralia](/wiki/Feralia "Feralia")) influenced All Saints' and All Souls' days.[[58]](#cite_note-60)[[59]](#cite_note-61) Parentalia involved a [commemorative meal](/wiki/Refrigerium "Refrigerium") at the graves of relatives, during which food and drink were offered to the dead, and Christian Romans continued this custom, extending it to the saints and martyrs.[[60]](#cite_note-62)[[61]](#cite_note-63)

There is evidence that by 800, churches in Ireland[[62]](#cite_note-farmer-64) and [Northumbria](/wiki/Northumbria "Northumbria") were holding a feast commemorating all saints on 1 November.[[63]](#cite_note-hutton364-65) In 835, the [Frankish Empire](/wiki/Frankish_Empire "Frankish Empire") officially adopted 1 November as the date of All Saints' Day.[[63]](#cite_note-hutton364-65) This may have been promoted by [Alcuin](/wiki/Alcuin "Alcuin") of Northumbria, who was a member of [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne")'s court,[[64]](#cite_note-66) or by the Irish clerics and scholars who were also members of the Frankish court.[[65]](#cite_note-Dales-67) Some suggest the date was due to Celtic influence; others, that it was a Germanic idea,[[63]](#cite_note-hutton364-65) although it is said that both Germanic- and Celtic-speaking peoples commemorated the dead at the beginning of winter.[[66]](#cite_note-macculloch10-68) They may have seen it as the most fitting time to do so, as it is a time of "dying" in nature.[[63]](#cite_note-hutton364-65)[[66]](#cite_note-macculloch10-68) It is also suggested the change was made on the "practical grounds that Rome in summer could not accommodate the great number of pilgrims who flocked to it", and perhaps because of [public health](/wiki/Public_health "Public health") concerns over [Roman Fever](/wiki/Roman_Fever_(disease) "Roman Fever (disease)"), which claimed a number of lives during Rome's sultry summers.[[67]](#cite_note-69)[[49]](#cite_note-Hopwood2019-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b7/Halloween_Bangladesh.jpg/250px-Halloween_Bangladesh.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halloween_Bangladesh.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d8/Halloween_Sweden.png/250px-Halloween_Sweden.png)](/wiki/File:Halloween_Sweden.png)

On All Hallows' Eve, Christians in some parts of the world visit cemeteries to pray and place flowers and candles on the graves of their loved ones.[[68]](#cite_note-70) Top: [Christians in Bangladesh](/wiki/Christianity_in_Bangladesh "Christianity in Bangladesh") lighting candles on the headstone of a relative. Bottom: [Lutheran Christians](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran") praying and lighting candles in front of the central crucifix of a graveyard.

All Souls' Day, a feast commemorating all deceased Christians, became widespread in the 12th century.[[69]](#cite_note-71) Its date was fixed on 2 November, the day after All Saints' Day. By the end of the 12th century, they had become [holy days of obligation](/wiki/Holy_day_of_obligation "Holy day of obligation") requiring church attendance in Western Christianity and involved such traditions as ringing [church bells](/wiki/Church_bell "Church bell") for souls in [Purgatory](/wiki/Purgatory "Purgatory"). It was also "customary for [criers](/wiki/Town_crier "Town crier") dressed in black to parade the streets, ringing a bell of mournful sound and calling on all good Christians to remember the poor souls".[[70]](#cite_note-72)

The Allhallowtide custom of baking and sharing [soul cakes](/wiki/Soul_cake "Soul cake") for all [christened](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism") souls[[71]](#cite_note-AFP-73) has been suggested as the origin of trick-or-treating.[[72]](#cite_note-Britannica-74) The custom dates back at least as far as the 15th century[[73]](#cite_note-hutton374-375-75) and was found in parts of England, Wales, Flanders, [Bavaria and Austria](/wiki/Allerheiligenstriezel "Allerheiligenstriezel").[[74]](#cite_note-miles-76) Groups of poor people, often children, would go door to door during Allhallowtide, collecting [soul cakes](/wiki/Soul_cake "Soul cake") in exchange for [praying for the dead](/wiki/Prayer_for_the_dead "Prayer for the dead"), especially the souls of the givers' friends and relatives. This was called "souling".[[73]](#cite_note-hutton374-375-75)[[75]](#cite_note-Dodge-77)[[76]](#cite_note-DeMello2012-78) Soul cakes were also offered for the souls themselves to eat,[[74]](#cite_note-miles-76) being laid on graves, or the "soulers" would act as their representatives.[[77]](#cite_note-79) As with the [Lenten](/wiki/Lent "Lent") tradition of [hot cross buns](/wiki/Hot_cross_bun "Hot cross bun"), soul cakes were often marked with a [cross](/wiki/Christian_cross "Christian cross"), indicating that they were baked as [alms](/wiki/Alms#Christianity "Alms").[[78]](#cite_note-Levene2016-80) [Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare") mentions souling in his comedy *[The Two Gentlemen of Verona](/wiki/The_Two_Gentlemen_of_Verona "The Two Gentlemen of Verona")* (1593).[[79]](#cite_note-81) While souling, Christians would carry "lanterns made of hollowed-out turnips", which could have originally represented souls of the dead;[[80]](#cite_note-PulliamFonseca2016-82)[[81]](#cite_note-rogers57-83) later [jack-o'-lanterns](/wiki/Jack-o%27-lanterns "Jack-o'-lanterns") were used to ward off evil spirits.[[82]](#cite_note-CarterPetro1998-84)[[83]](#cite_note-Guiley2008-85)

Christian minister Prince Sorie Conteh linked the wearing of costumes to the belief in [vengeful ghosts](/wiki/Vengeful_ghost "Vengeful ghost"): "It was traditionally believed that the souls of the departed wandered the earth until All Saints' Day, and All Hallows' Eve provided one last chance for the dead to gain vengeance on their enemies before moving to the [next world](/wiki/Christian_eschatology "Christian eschatology"). In order to avoid being recognized by any soul that might be seeking such vengeance, people would don masks or costumes".[[84]](#cite_note-Christian-86) In the Middle Ages, churches in Europe that were too poor to display [relics](/wiki/Relic "Relic") of [martyred saints](/wiki/Christian_martyr "Christian martyr") at Allhallowtide let parishioners dress up as saints instead.[[85]](#cite_note-Bannatyne1998-87)[[86]](#cite_note-Morrow2001-88) Some Christians observe this custom at Halloween today.[[87]](#cite_note-89) [Lesley Bannatyne](/wiki/Lesley_Bannatyne "Lesley Bannatyne") believes this could have been a Christianization of an earlier pagan custom.[[88]](#cite_note-bannatyne9-90) Many Christians in mainland Europe, especially in France, believed "that once a year, on Hallowe'en, the dead of the churchyards rose for one wild, hideous carnival" known as the *[danse macabre](/wiki/Danse_macabre "Danse macabre")*, which was often depicted in [church decoration](/wiki/Christian_art "Christian art").[[89]](#cite_note-91) [Christopher Allmand](/wiki/Christopher_Allmand "Christopher Allmand") and [Rosamond McKitterick](/wiki/Rosamond_McKitterick "Rosamond McKitterick") write in *The New Cambridge Medieval History* that the *[danse macabre](/wiki/Danse_Macabre "Danse Macabre")* urged Christians "not to forget the end of all earthly things".[[90]](#cite_note-Allmand1998-92) The *danse macabre* was sometimes enacted in European village pageants and [court masques](/wiki/Masque "Masque") with people "dressing up as corpses from various strata of society", and this may be the origin of Halloween costume parties.[[91]](#cite_note-Reimer2018-93)[[92]](#cite_note-DeSpelderStrickland2009-94)[[93]](#cite_note-95)[[80]](#cite_note-PulliamFonseca2016-82)

In Britain, these customs came under attack during the [Reformation](/wiki/English_Reformation "English Reformation"), as [Protestants](/wiki/Protestant "Protestant") berated Purgatory as a "[popish](/wiki/Popish "Popish")" doctrine incompatible with the Calvinist doctrine of [predestination](/wiki/Predestination "Predestination"). State-sanctioned ceremonies associated with the [intercession of saints](/wiki/Intercession_of_saints "Intercession of saints") and [prayer for souls in Purgatory](/wiki/Prayer_for_the_dead "Prayer for the dead") were abolished during the [Elizabethan reform](/wiki/Elizabethan_Religious_Settlement "Elizabethan Religious Settlement"), though All Hallows' Day remained in the English [liturgical calendar](/wiki/Liturgical_calendar "Liturgical calendar") to "commemorate saints as godly human beings".[[94]](#cite_note-96) For some [Nonconformist Protestants](/wiki/Nonconformist_(Protestantism) "Nonconformist (Protestantism)"), the [theology](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology") of All Hallows' Eve was redefined: "souls cannot be journeying from Purgatory on their way to Heaven, as Catholics frequently believe and assert. Instead, the so-called ghosts are thought to be in actuality evil spirits".[[95]](#cite_note-97) Other Protestants believed in an [intermediate state](/wiki/Intermediate_state_(Christianity) "Intermediate state (Christianity)") known as [Hades](/wiki/Christian_views_on_Hades "Christian views on Hades") ([Bosom of Abraham](/wiki/Bosom_of_Abraham "Bosom of Abraham")).[[96]](#cite_note-98) In some localities, Catholics and Protestants continued souling, [candlelit](/wiki/Votive_candle "Votive candle") [processions](/wiki/Procession "Procession"), or ringing church bells for the dead;[[51]](#cite_note-rogers_religion-53)[[97]](#cite_note-Mosteller-99) but the Anglican Church eventually suppressed this bell-ringing.[[98]](#cite_note-100) Professor of [medieval archaeology](/wiki/Medieval_archaeology "Medieval archaeology") Mark Donnelly and historian Daniel Diehl write that "barns and [homes were blessed](/wiki/House_blessing "House blessing") to protect people and livestock from the [effect of witches](/wiki/Christian_views_on_magic "Christian views on magic"), who were believed to accompany the [malignant spirits](/wiki/Unclean_spirit "Unclean spirit") as they traveled the earth".[[99]](#cite_note-Medieval_Celebrations_page_17-101)

After 1605, Allhallowtide was eclipsed in England by [Guy Fawkes Night](/wiki/Guy_Fawkes_Night "Guy Fawkes Night") (5 November), which appropriated some of its customs.[[100]](#cite_note-rogers_kirk-102) In England, the ending of official ceremonies related to the intercession of saints led to the development of new, unofficial Allhallowtide customs. In 18th- and 19th-century rural [Lancashire](/wiki/Lancashire "Lancashire"), Catholic families gathered on hills on the night of All Hallows' Eve and one person held a bunch of burning straw on a [pitchfork](/wiki/Pitchfork "Pitchfork") while the rest knelt around him, praying for the souls of relatives and friends until the flames went out. This was known as *teen'lay*.[[101]](#cite_note-Hutton2001-103) There was a similar custom in [Hertfordshire](/wiki/Hertfordshire "Hertfordshire"), and the lighting of "tindle" fires in [Derbyshire](/wiki/Derbyshire "Derbyshire").[[102]](#cite_note-104) Some suggested that these fires were originally lit to "guide the poor souls back to earth".[[103]](#cite_note-105) In Scotland and Ireland, old Allhallowtide customs that were at odds with Reformed teaching were not suppressed because they "were important to the life cycle and rites of passage of local communities" and so curbing them would have been difficult.[[25]](#cite_note-rogers_nw-27)

On All Saints' Day and All Souls' Day during the 19th century, candles were lit in homes in Ireland,[[104]](#cite_note-Santino,_p.95-106) Flanders, Bavaria, and in [Tyrol](/wiki/Tyrol "Tyrol"), where they were called "soul lights",[[105]](#cite_note-Frazer_All_Souls-107) that served "to guide the souls back to visit their earthly homes".[[106]](#cite_note-108) Across Christendom, in preparation for Allhallowtide, Christians flocked to cemeteries "decorating the graves of their dear ones with flowers, tending the lawn, and spreading fresh white gravel around the tombs" with "candles, protected by little glass lanterns" being "placed around the graves or at the foot of the tombstones, to be lighted on All Saints' eve and left burning prior through the night."[[107]](#cite_note-Weiser1956-109) The [use of candles by Christians](/wiki/Ceremonial_use_of_lights#Christianity "Ceremonial use of lights") symbolized the [light of Christ](/wiki/Light_of_Christ "Light of Christ") and the use of lamps at the tombs of [Christian martyrs](/wiki/Christian_martyrs "Christian martyrs") dates back to the early Christian period.[[108]](#cite_note-110)[[109]](#cite_note-Beeton-111)

In 19th century [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany"), [libations](/wiki/Libation "Libation") of milk were poured on the graves of kinfolk,[[74]](#cite_note-miles-76) or food would be left overnight on the dinner table for the returning souls;[[105]](#cite_note-Frazer_All_Souls-107) a custom also found in Tyrol and parts of Italy.[[110]](#cite_note-Morton2013-112)[[105]](#cite_note-Frazer_All_Souls-107) In [Salerno](/wiki/Salerno "Salerno"), until the 15th century, families left a meal out for the [ghosts](/wiki/Ghost#Judæo-Christian "Ghost") of relatives before leaving for [church services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service").[[110]](#cite_note-Morton2013-112) In 19th century Bavaria, food was laid on graves to feed the souls of the dead.[[105]](#cite_note-Frazer_All_Souls-107)

In 19th-century Italy, churches staged "theatrical re-enactments of scenes from the lives of the saints" on All Hallows' Day, with "participants represented by realistic wax figures".[[110]](#cite_note-Morton2013-112) In 1823, the graveyard of [Holy Spirit Hospital](/wiki/Ospedale_di_Santo_Spirito_in_Sassia "Ospedale di Santo Spirito in Sassia") in Rome presented a scene in which bodies of those who recently died were arrayed around a wax statue of an [angel](/wiki/Christian_angelology "Christian angelology") who pointed upward towards [heaven](/wiki/Heaven_in_Christianity "Heaven in Christianity").[[110]](#cite_note-Morton2013-112) In the same country, "parish priests went house-to-house, asking for small gifts of food which they shared among themselves throughout that night".[[110]](#cite_note-Morton2013-112)

In 19th-century Spain at Allhallowtide, there was a procession in the city of [San Sebastián](/wiki/San_Sebasti%C3%A1n "San Sebastián") to the city cemetery, an event that drew beggars who "appeal[ed] to the tender recollections of one's deceased relations and friends" for sympathy.[[111]](#cite_note-Ford1855-113) People in Spain continue to bake special pastries called "bones of the holy" (Spanish: *huesos de santo*) and set them on graves;[[112]](#cite_note-The_Halloween_Encyclopedia_page_9-114) and at cemeteries in both Spain and France, as well as in [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"), priests lead [Christian processions](/wiki/Christian_procession "Christian procession") and services during Allhallowtide, after which people keep an all-night vigil.[[113]](#cite_note-2017Fieldhouse-115)

### Celtic folk influence

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1e/Traditional-irish-halloween-mask.jpg/250px-Traditional-irish-halloween-mask.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traditional-irish-halloween-mask.jpg)

An early 20th-century Irish Halloween mask displayed at the [Museum of Country Life](/wiki/Museum_of_Country_Life "Museum of Country Life") in [County Mayo](/wiki/County_Mayo "County Mayo"), Ireland

Today's Halloween customs are thought to have been influenced by [folk](/wiki/Folk_culture "Folk culture") customs and beliefs from the [Celtic-speaking countries](/wiki/Celtic_nations "Celtic nations"), some of which are believed to have [pagan](/wiki/Celtic_polytheism "Celtic polytheism") roots.[[114]](#cite_note-116) [Jack Santino](/wiki/Jack_Santino "Jack Santino"), a [folklorist](/wiki/Folkloristics "Folkloristics"), writes that "there was throughout Ireland an uneasy truce existing between customs and beliefs associated with Christianity and those associated with religions that were Irish before Christianity arrived".[[115]](#cite_note-117) The origins of Halloween customs are typically linked to the [Gaelic](/wiki/Gaels "Gaels") festival [Samhain](/wiki/Samhain "Samhain").[[116]](#cite_note-rogers_s-118)

Samhain is one of the "[quarter days](/wiki/Quarter_days "Quarter days")" in the medieval Gaelic calendar and has been celebrated on 31 October – 1 November[[117]](#cite_note-119) in Ireland, Scotland and the [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man").[[118]](#cite_note-Roud-120)[[119]](#cite_note-bbc_halloween-121) A kindred festival has been held by the [Brittonic](/wiki/Brittonic_languages "Brittonic languages") Celts, called *[Calan Gaeaf](/wiki/Calan_Gaeaf "Calan Gaeaf")* in Wales, *[Kalan Gwav](/wiki/Allantide "Allantide")* in [Cornwall](/wiki/Cornwall "Cornwall") and *Kalan Goañv* in [Brittany](/wiki/Brittany "Brittany"): a name meaning "first day of winter". For the Celts, the day ended and began at sunset; thus the festival begins the evening before 1 November by modern reckoning.[[120]](#cite_note-122) Samhain is mentioned in some of the earliest Irish literature. The names have been used by historians to refer to Celtic Halloween customs up until the 19th century,[[121]](#cite_note-hutton365-369-123) and are still the Gaelic and Welsh names for Halloween.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Snap-Apple_Night_globalphilosophy.PNG/250px-Snap-Apple_Night_globalphilosophy.PNG)](/wiki/File:Snap-Apple_Night_globalphilosophy.PNG)

*Snap-Apple Night, or All-Hallow Eve*, painted by [Daniel Maclise](/wiki/Daniel_Maclise "Daniel Maclise") in 1833, shows people feasting and playing divination games on Halloween in Ireland.[[122]](#cite_note-124)

Samhain marked the end of the [harvest](/wiki/Harvest "Harvest") season in autumn and beginning of winter or the 'darker half' of the year.[[123]](#cite_note-monaghan407-125)[[124]](#cite_note-126) It was seen as a [liminal](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/liminal "wikt:liminal") time, when the boundary between this world and the [Otherworld](/wiki/Celtic_Otherworld "Celtic Otherworld") thinned. This meant the *[Aos Sí](/wiki/Aos_S%C3%AD "Aos Sí")*, the 'spirits' or '[fairies](/wiki/Fairy "Fairy")', could more easily come into this world and were particularly active.[[125]](#cite_note-127)[[126]](#cite_note-128) Most scholars see them as "degraded versions of ancient gods [...] whose power remained active in the people's minds even after they had been officially replaced by later religious beliefs".[[127]](#cite_note-129) They were both respected and feared, with individuals often invoking the protection of [God](/wiki/God_in_Christianity "God in Christianity") when approaching their dwellings.[[128]](#cite_note-Monaghan2009-130)[[129]](#cite_note-131) At Samhain, the *Aos Sí* were [appeased](/wiki/Propitiation "Propitiation") to ensure the people and livestock survived the winter. Offerings of food and drink, or portions of the crops, were left outside for them.[[130]](#cite_note-132)[[131]](#cite_note-133)[[132]](#cite_note-134) The souls of the dead were also said to revisit their homes seeking [hospitality](/wiki/Hospitality "Hospitality").[[133]](#cite_note-135) Places were set at the dinner table and by the fire to welcome them.[[134]](#cite_note-mcneill1-136) The belief that the souls of the dead return home on one night of the year and must be appeased seems to have ancient origins and is found in many cultures.[[74]](#cite_note-miles-76) In 19th century Ireland, "candles would be lit and [prayers](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") formally offered for the souls of the dead. After this the eating, drinking, and games would begin".[[135]](#cite_note-137)

Throughout Ireland and Britain, especially in the Celtic-speaking regions, the household festivities included [divination](/wiki/Divination "Divination") rituals and games intended to foretell one's future, especially regarding death and marriage.[[136]](#cite_note-Hutton,_p.380-138) Apples and nuts were often used, and customs included [apple bobbing](/wiki/Apple_bobbing "Apple bobbing"), nut roasting, [scrying](/wiki/Scrying "Scrying") or mirror-gazing, [pouring molten lead](/wiki/Molybdomancy "Molybdomancy") or [egg whites](/wiki/Oomancy "Oomancy") into water, [dream interpretation](/wiki/Dream_interpretation "Dream interpretation"), and others.[[137]](#cite_note-139) Special [bonfires](/wiki/Bonfire "Bonfire") were lit and there were rituals involving them. Their flames, smoke, and ashes were deemed to have protective and cleansing powers.[[123]](#cite_note-monaghan407-125) In some places, torches lit from the bonfire were carried [sunwise](/wiki/Sunwise "Sunwise") around homes and fields to protect them.[[121]](#cite_note-hutton365-369-123) It is suggested the fires were a kind of [imitative or sympathetic magic](/wiki/Sympathetic_magic "Sympathetic magic") – they mimicked the Sun and held back the decay and darkness of winter.[[134]](#cite_note-mcneill1-136)[[138]](#cite_note-frazer63-140)[[139]](#cite_note-macculloch-141) They were also used for divination and to ward off evil spirits.[[82]](#cite_note-CarterPetro1998-84) In Scotland, these bonfires and divination games were banned by the church elders in some parishes.[[140]](#cite_note-142) In Wales, bonfires were also lit to "prevent the souls of the dead from falling to earth".[[141]](#cite_note-143) Later, these bonfires "kept away the [devil](/wiki/Devil_in_Christianity "Devil in Christianity")".[[142]](#cite_note-Rosinsky2002-144)

[![A plaster cast of a traditional Irish Halloween turnip](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Traditional_Irish_halloween_Jack-o%27-lantern.jpg/250px-Traditional_Irish_halloween_Jack-o%27-lantern.jpg)](/wiki/File:Traditional_Irish_halloween_Jack-o%27-lantern.jpg)

A plaster cast of a traditional Irish Halloween turnip (swede, [rutabaga](/wiki/Rutabaga "Rutabaga")) lantern on display in the [Museum of Country Life](/wiki/Museum_of_Country_Life "Museum of Country Life"), Ireland[[143]](#cite_note-145)

From at least the 16th century,[[144]](#cite_note-mcneill2-146) the festival included [mumming](/wiki/Mummers_Play "Mummers Play") and [guising](/wiki/Guising "Guising") in Ireland, Scotland, the Isle of Man and Wales.[[145]](#cite_note-hutton379-383-147) This involved people going house-to-house in costume (or in disguise), usually reciting verses or songs in exchange for food. It may have originally been a tradition whereby people impersonated the *Aos Sí*, or the souls of the dead, and received offerings on their behalf, similar to '[souling](/wiki/Soul_cake "Soul cake")'. Impersonating these beings, or wearing a disguise, was also believed to protect oneself from them.[[146]](#cite_note-148) In parts of southern Ireland, the guisers included a [hobby horse](/wiki/Hobby_horse "Hobby horse"). A man dressed as a *láir bhán* ([white mare](/wiki/White_horse_(mythology) "White horse (mythology)")) led youths house-to-house reciting verses – some of which had pagan overtones – in exchange for food. If the household donated food it could expect good fortune from the 'Muck Olla'; not doing so would bring misfortune.[[147]](#cite_note-149) In Scotland, youths went house-to-house with masked, painted or blackened faces, often threatening to do mischief if they were not welcomed.[[145]](#cite_note-hutton379-383-147) [F. Marian McNeill](/wiki/F._Marian_McNeill "F. Marian McNeill") suggests the ancient festival included people in costume representing the spirits, and that faces were marked or blackened with ashes from the sacred bonfire.[[144]](#cite_note-mcneill2-146) In parts of Wales, men went about dressed as fearsome beings called *gwrachod*.[[145]](#cite_note-hutton379-383-147) In the late 19th and early 20th century, young people in [Glamorgan](/wiki/Glamorgan "Glamorgan") and [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney") [cross-dressed](/wiki/Cross-dressing "Cross-dressing").[[145]](#cite_note-hutton379-383-147)

Elsewhere in Europe, mumming was part of other festivals, but in the Celtic-speaking regions, it was "particularly appropriate to a night upon which supernatural beings were said to be abroad and could be imitated or warded off by human wanderers".[[145]](#cite_note-hutton379-383-147) From at least the 18th century, "imitating malignant spirits" led to playing pranks in Ireland and the [Scottish Highlands](/wiki/Scottish_Highlands "Scottish Highlands"). Wearing costumes and playing pranks at Halloween did not spread to England until the 20th century.[[145]](#cite_note-hutton379-383-147) Pranksters used hollowed-out [turnips](/wiki/Rutabaga "Rutabaga") or [mangel wurzels](/wiki/Mangelwurzel "Mangelwurzel") as lanterns, often carved with grotesque faces.[[145]](#cite_note-hutton379-383-147) By those who made them, the lanterns were variously said to represent the spirits,[[145]](#cite_note-hutton379-383-147) or [used to ward off](/wiki/Apotropaic_magic "Apotropaic magic") evil spirits.[[148]](#cite_note-palmer87-150)[[149]](#cite_note-151) They were common in parts of Ireland and the Scottish Highlands in the 19th century,[[145]](#cite_note-hutton379-383-147) as well as in [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset "Somerset") (see [Punkie Night](/wiki/Punkie_Night "Punkie Night")). In the 20th century they spread to other parts of Britain and became generally known as [jack-o'-lanterns](/wiki/Jack-o%27-lantern "Jack-o'-lantern").[[145]](#cite_note-hutton379-383-147)

### Spread to North America

[Lesley Bannatyne](/wiki/Lesley_Bannatyne "Lesley Bannatyne") and Cindy Ott write that [Anglican](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") colonists in the southern United States and [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") colonists in [Maryland](/wiki/Maryland "Maryland") "recognized All Hallows' Eve in their church calendars",[[150]](#cite_note-152)[[151]](#cite_note-153) although the Puritans of [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") strongly opposed the holiday, along with other traditional celebrations of the established Church, including Christmas.[[152]](#cite_note-154) [Almanacs](/wiki/Almanac "Almanac") of the late 18th and early 19th century give no indication that Halloween was widely celebrated in North America.[[25]](#cite_note-rogers_nw-27)

It was not until after mass [Irish](/wiki/Irish_diaspora "Irish diaspora") and [Scottish immigration](/wiki/Scottish_diaspora "Scottish diaspora") in the 19th century that Halloween became a major holiday in America.[[25]](#cite_note-rogers_nw-27) Most American Halloween traditions were inherited from the Irish and Scots,[[26]](#cite_note-Brunvand,_Jan_2006._p.749-28)[[153]](#cite_note-155) though "In [Cajun](/wiki/Cajun "Cajun") areas, a nocturnal Mass was said in cemeteries on Halloween night. Candles that had been blessed were placed on graves, and families sometimes spent the entire night at the graveside".[[154]](#cite_note-Morton2003-156) Originally confined to these immigrant communities, it was gradually assimilated into mainstream society and was celebrated coast to coast by people of all social, racial, and religious backgrounds by the early 20th century.[[155]](#cite_note-rogers_nationwide-157) Then, through [American influence](/wiki/Americanization "Americanization"), these Halloween traditions spread to many other countries by the late 20th and early 21st century, including to [mainland](/wiki/Continental_Europe "Continental Europe") Europe and some parts of the [Far East](/wiki/Far_East "Far East").[[27]](#cite_note-Colavito,_Jason_2007._pp.151-29)[[14]](#cite_note-rogers_m-16)[[156]](#cite_note-158)

## Symbols

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Halloween_Witch_2011.JPG/250px-Halloween_Witch_2011.JPG)](/wiki/File:Halloween_Witch_2011.JPG)

At Halloween, yards, public spaces, and some houses may be decorated with traditionally macabre symbols including [skeletons](/wiki/Skeleton_(undead) "Skeleton (undead)"), [ghosts](/wiki/Ghost "Ghost"), [cobwebs](/wiki/Spider_web "Spider web"), [headstones](/wiki/Headstone "Headstone"), [witches](/wiki/Witchcraft "Witchcraft") and [scarecrows](/wiki/Scarecrows "Scarecrows")

Development of [artifacts](/wiki/Artefact_(archaeology) "Artefact (archaeology)") and [symbols](/wiki/Symbol "Symbol") associated with Halloween formed over time. [Jack-o'-lanterns](/wiki/Jack-o%27-lantern "Jack-o'-lantern") are traditionally carried by [guisers](/wiki/Guising "Guising") on All Hallows' Eve in order to frighten [evil spirits](/wiki/Unclean_spirit "Unclean spirit").[[81]](#cite_note-rogers57-83)[[157]](#cite_note-159) There is a popular [Irish Christian](/wiki/Christianity_in_Ireland "Christianity in Ireland") folktale associated with the jack-o'-lantern,[[158]](#cite_note-160) which in [folklore](/wiki/Folklore "Folklore") is said to represent a "[soul](/wiki/Soul_in_the_Bible "Soul in the Bible") who has been denied entry into both [heaven](/wiki/Heaven_(Christianity) "Heaven (Christianity)") and [hell](/wiki/Christian_views_on_Hell "Christian views on Hell")":[[159]](#cite_note-161)

> On route home after a night's drinking, [Jack](/wiki/Stingy_Jack "Stingy Jack") encounters the [Devil](/wiki/Devil_in_Christianity "Devil in Christianity") and tricks him into climbing a tree. A quick-thinking Jack etches the [sign of the cross](/wiki/Sign_of_the_cross "Sign of the cross") into the bark, thus trapping the Devil. Jack strikes a bargain that [Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan") can never claim his soul. After a life of [sin](/wiki/Christian_views_on_sin "Christian views on sin"), [drink](/wiki/Christian_views_on_alcohol "Christian views on alcohol"), and mendacity, Jack is refused entry to heaven when he dies. Keeping his promise, the Devil refuses to let Jack into hell and throws a live coal straight from the fires of hell at him. It was a cold night, so Jack places the coal in a hollowed out turnip to stop it from going out, since which time Jack and his lantern have been roaming looking for a place to rest.[[160]](#cite_note-162)

In Ireland, Scotland, and Northern England the [turnip](/wiki/Neep "Neep") has traditionally been carved during Halloween,[[161]](#cite_note-lant-163)[[162]](#cite_note-frle-164) but immigrants to North America used the native pumpkin, which is both much softer and much larger, making it easier to carve than a turnip.[[161]](#cite_note-lant-163) The American tradition of carving pumpkins is recorded in 1837[[163]](#cite_note-165) and was originally associated with harvest time in general, not becoming specifically associated with Halloween until the mid-to-late 19th century.[[164]](#cite_note-166)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/J._M._Wright_-_Edward_Scriven_-_Robert_Burns_-_Halloween.JPG/250px-J._M._Wright_-_Edward_Scriven_-_Robert_Burns_-_Halloween.JPG)](/wiki/File:J._M._Wright_-_Edward_Scriven_-_Robert_Burns_-_Halloween.JPG)

"[Halloween](/wiki/Halloween_(poem) "Halloween (poem)")" (1785) by Scottish poet [Robert Burns](/wiki/Robert_Burns "Robert Burns"), recounts various legends of the holiday.

The modern imagery of Halloween comes from many sources, including [Christian eschatology](/wiki/Christian_eschatology "Christian eschatology"), national customs, works of [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_fiction "Gothic fiction") and [horror](/wiki/Horror_fiction "Horror fiction") literature (such as the novels *[Frankenstein; or, The Modern Prometheus](/wiki/Frankenstein "Frankenstein")* and *[Dracula](/wiki/Dracula "Dracula")*) and classic horror films such as *[Frankenstein](/wiki/Frankenstein_(1931_film) "Frankenstein (1931 film)")* (1931) and *[Night of the Living Dead](/wiki/Night_of_the_Living_Dead_(1968_film) "Night of the Living Dead (1968 film)")* (1968).[[165]](#cite_note-167)[[166]](#cite_note-rogers_h-168) Imagery of the [skull](/wiki/Skull_and_crossbones_(Spanish_cemetery) "Skull and crossbones (Spanish cemetery)"), a reference to [Golgotha](/wiki/Golgotha "Golgotha") in the Christian tradition, serves as "a reminder of death and the transitory quality of human life" and is consequently found in *[memento mori](/wiki/Memento_mori "Memento mori")* and *[vanitas](/wiki/Vanitas "Vanitas")* compositions;[[167]](#cite_note-169) skulls have therefore been commonplace in Halloween, which touches on this theme.[[168]](#cite_note-170) Traditionally, the back walls of churches are "decorated with a depiction of the [Last Judgment](/wiki/Last_Judgment "Last Judgment"), complete with graves opening and the dead rising, with a heaven filled with angels and a hell filled with devils", a motif that has permeated the observance of this [triduum](/wiki/Allhallowtide "Allhallowtide").[[169]](#cite_note-171) One of the earliest works on the subject of Halloween is from Scottish poet [John Mayne](/wiki/John_Mayne "John Mayne"), who, in 1780, made note of [pranks](/wiki/Prank "Prank") at Halloween—*"What fearfu' pranks ensue!"*, as well as the supernatural associated with the night, *"bogles"* (ghosts)[[170]](#cite_note-172)—influencing [Robert Burns](/wiki/Robert_Burns "Robert Burns")' "[Halloween](/wiki/Halloween_(poem) "Halloween (poem)")" (1785).[[171]](#cite_note-173) Elements of the [autumn season](/wiki/Autumn "Autumn"), such as pumpkins, corn [husks](/wiki/Husk "Husk"), autumn [leaves](/wiki/Leaves "Leaves"), and [scarecrows](/wiki/Scarecrow "Scarecrow"), are also prevalent. Homes are often decorated with these types of symbols around Halloween. Halloween imagery includes themes of death, [evil](/wiki/Evil "Evil"), and mythical [monsters](/wiki/Monster "Monster").[[172]](#cite_note-174) [Black cats](/wiki/Black_cat "Black cat"), which have been long associated with witches, are also a common symbol of Halloween. Black, orange, and sometimes purple are Halloween's traditional colors.[[173]](#cite_note-175)

## Trick-or-treating and guising

Main article: [Trick-or-treating](/wiki/Trick-or-treating "Trick-or-treating")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f3/Trick_or_treat_in_sweden.jpeg/250px-Trick_or_treat_in_sweden.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Trick_or_treat_in_sweden.jpeg)

Trick-or-treaters in Sweden

Trick-or-treating is a customary celebration for children on Halloween. Children go in costume from house to house, asking for treats such as [candy](/wiki/Confectionery "Confectionery") and other confections, or sometimes money, with the question, "Trick or treat?" The word "treat" is asking for a sweet treat while the word "trick" implies a "threat" to perform mischief on the homeowners or their property if no treat is given.[[72]](#cite_note-Britannica-74) The practice is said to have roots in the medieval practice of [mumming](/wiki/Mumming "Mumming"), which is closely related to [souling](/wiki/Souling "Souling").[[174]](#cite_note-176) John Pymm wrote that "many of the feast days associated with the presentation of mumming plays were celebrated by the Christian Church."[[175]](#cite_note-177) These [feast days](/wiki/Feast_day "Feast day") included All Hallows' Eve, Christmas, [Twelfth Night](/wiki/Twelfth_Night_(holiday) "Twelfth Night (holiday)") and [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday").[[176]](#cite_note-178)[[177]](#cite_note-179) Mumming practiced in Germany, Scandinavia and other parts of Europe,[[178]](#cite_note-180) involved masked persons in [fancy dress](/wiki/Costume_party "Costume party") who "paraded the streets and entered houses to dance or play dice in silence".[[179]](#cite_note-181)

In England, from the medieval period,[[180]](#cite_note-Carmichael2012-182) up until the 1930s,[[181]](#cite_note-Hood2014-183) people practiced the Christian custom of souling on Halloween, which involved groups of soulers, both Protestant and Catholic,[[97]](#cite_note-Mosteller-99) going from [parish](/wiki/Parish_(Church_of_England) "Parish (Church of England)") to parish, begging the rich for soul cakes, in exchange for [praying](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") for the souls of the givers and their friends.[[75]](#cite_note-Dodge-77) In the Philippines, the practice of souling is called [Pangangaluluwa](/wiki/Pangangaluluwa "Pangangaluluwa") and is practiced on All Hallows' Eve among children in rural areas.[[28]](#cite_note-Fieldhouse2017p256-30) People drape themselves in white cloths to represent souls and then visit houses, where they sing in return for prayers and sweets.[[28]](#cite_note-Fieldhouse2017p256-30)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Waterdown_Public_School%2C_Ontario%2C_1928_halloween_costume.jpg/250px-Waterdown_Public_School%2C_Ontario%2C_1928_halloween_costume.jpg)](/wiki/File:Waterdown_Public_School,_Ontario,_1928_halloween_costume.jpg)

Girl in a Halloween costume in 1928, [Ontario](/wiki/Ontario "Ontario"), Canada, the same province where the Scottish Halloween custom of [guising](/wiki/Guising "Guising") was first recorded in North America

In Scotland and Ireland, [guising](/wiki/Guising "Guising")—children disguised in costume going from door to door for food or coins—is a secular Halloween custom.[[182]](#cite_note-Irish_Times-184) It is recorded in Scotland at Halloween in 1895 where masqueraders in disguise carrying lanterns made out of scooped out turnips, visit homes to be rewarded with cakes, fruit, and money.[[162]](#cite_note-frle-164)[[183]](#cite_note-Definition-185) In Ireland, the most popular phrase for kids to shout (until the 2000s) was "[Help the Halloween Party](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/help_the_Halloween_party "wikt:help the Halloween party")".[[182]](#cite_note-Irish_Times-184) Author Nicholas Rogers cites an early example of guising in North America in 1911, where a newspaper in [Kingston, Ontario](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario"), Canada, reported children going "guising" around the neighborhood.[[184]](#cite_note-186)

American historian and author [Ruth Edna Kelley](/wiki/Ruth_Edna_Kelley "Ruth Edna Kelley") of [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") wrote the first book-length history of Halloween in the US: *[The Book of Hallowe'en](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/The_Book_of_Hallowe%27en "s:The Book of Hallowe'en")* (1919), and references souling in the chapter "Hallowe'en in America".[[185]](#cite_note-187) In her book, Kelley touches on customs that arrived from across the Atlantic; "Americans have fostered them, and are making this an occasion something like what it must have been in its best days overseas. All Halloween customs in the United States are borrowed directly or adapted from those of other countries".[[186]](#cite_note-188)

While the first reference to "guising" in North America occurs in 1911, another reference to ritual begging on Halloween appears, place unknown, in 1915, with a third reference in Chicago in 1920.[[187]](#cite_note-189) The earliest known use in print of the term "trick or treat" appears in 1917, in *[The Sault Daily Star](/wiki/The_Sault_Star "The Sault Star")*, of [Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario](/wiki/Sault_Ste._Marie,_Ontario "Sault Ste. Marie, Ontario"), Canada.[[188]](#cite_note-Sault-190)

The thousands of [Halloween postcards](/wiki/Halloween_card "Halloween card") produced between the turn of the 20th century and the 1920s commonly show children but not trick-or-treating.[[189]](#cite_note-191) Trick-or-treating does not seem to have become a widespread practice in North America until the 1930s, with the first US appearances of the term in 1934,[[190]](#cite_note-192) and the first use in a national publication occurring in 1939.[[191]](#cite_note-193)

A popular variant of trick-or-treating, known as [trunk-or-treating](/wiki/Trunk-or-treating "Trunk-or-treating") (or Halloween tailgating), occurs when "children are offered treats from the trunks of cars parked in a church parking lot", or sometimes, a school parking lot.[[112]](#cite_note-The_Halloween_Encyclopedia_page_9-114)[[192]](#cite_note-194) In a trunk-or-treat event, the [trunk](/wiki/Trunk_(automobile) "Trunk (automobile)") (boot) of each automobile is decorated with a certain theme,[[193]](#cite_note-195) such as those of children's literature, movies, [scripture](/wiki/Bible "Bible"), and [job roles](/wiki/Job_(role) "Job (role)").[[194]](#cite_note-196) Trunk-or-treating has grown in popularity due to its perception as being more safe than going door to door, a point that resonates well with parents, as well as the fact that it "solves the rural conundrum in which homes [are] built a half-mile apart".[[195]](#cite_note-197)[[196]](#cite_note-198)

## Costumes

Main article: [Halloween costume](/wiki/Halloween_costume "Halloween costume")

Halloween costumes were traditionally modeled after figures such as [vampires](/wiki/Vampires "Vampires"), [ghosts](/wiki/Ghosts "Ghosts"), [skeletons](/wiki/Skeleton_(undead) "Skeleton (undead)"), scary looking [witches](/wiki/Witches "Witches"), and [scarecrows](/wiki/Scarecrow "Scarecrow").[[72]](#cite_note-Britannica-74) Over time, the costume selection extended to include popular characters from fiction, celebrities, and generic [archetypes](/wiki/Archetypes "Archetypes") such as [ninjas](/wiki/Ninja "Ninja") and [princesses](/wiki/Princess "Princess").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8a/Halloween_Shop%2C_Derry%2C_September_2010_%2802%29.JPG/250px-Halloween_Shop%2C_Derry%2C_September_2010_%2802%29.JPG)](/wiki/File:Halloween_Shop,_Derry,_September_2010_(02).JPG)

Halloween shop in [Derry](/wiki/Derry "Derry"), Northern Ireland, selling masks

Dressing up in costumes and going "[guising](/wiki/Guising "Guising")" was prevalent in Scotland and Ireland at Halloween by the late 19th century.[[162]](#cite_note-frle-164) A Scottish term, the tradition is called "guising" because of the disguises or costumes worn by the children.[[183]](#cite_note-Definition-185) In Ireland and Scotland, the masks are known as 'false faces',[[46]](#cite_note-DSL-48)[[197]](#cite_note-199) a term recorded in Ayr, Scotland in 1890 by a Scot describing guisers: "I had mind it was Halloween ... the wee callans (boys) were at it already, rinning aboot wi' their fause-faces (false faces) on and their bits o' turnip lanthrons (lanterns) in their haun (hand)".[[46]](#cite_note-DSL-48) Costuming became popular for Halloween parties in the US in the early 20th century, as often for adults as for children, and when trick-or-treating was becoming popular in Canada and the US in the 1920s and 1930s.[[188]](#cite_note-Sault-190)[[198]](#cite_note-200)

Eddie J. Smith, in his book *Halloween, Hallowed is Thy Name*, offers a religious perspective to the wearing of costumes on All Hallows' Eve, suggesting that by dressing up as creatures "who at one time caused us to fear and tremble", people are able to poke fun at [Satan](/wiki/Satan "Satan") "whose kingdom has been plundered by our Saviour". Images of skeletons and the dead are traditional decorations used as *[memento mori](/wiki/Memento_mori "Memento mori")*.[[199]](#cite_note-201)[[200]](#cite_note-202)

"[Trick-or-Treat for UNICEF](/wiki/Trick-or-Treat_for_UNICEF "Trick-or-Treat for UNICEF")" is a fundraising program to support [UNICEF](/wiki/UNICEF "UNICEF"),[[72]](#cite_note-Britannica-74) a United Nations Programme that provides humanitarian aid to children in developing countries. Started as a local event in a [Northeast Philadelphia](/wiki/Northeast_Philadelphia "Northeast Philadelphia") neighborhood in 1950 and expanded nationally in 1952, the program involves the distribution of small boxes by schools (or in modern times, corporate sponsors like [Hallmark](/wiki/Hallmark_Cards "Hallmark Cards"), at their licensed stores) to trick-or-treaters, in which they can solicit small-change donations from the houses they visit. It is estimated that children have collected more than $118 million for UNICEF since its inception. In Canada, in 2006, UNICEF decided to discontinue their Halloween collection boxes, citing safety and administrative concerns; after consultation with schools, they instead redesigned the program.[[201]](#cite_note-ctv-203)[[202]](#cite_note-ca_un-204)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/44/Greenwich_Village_Halloween_Parade_%286451249051%29.jpg/330px-Greenwich_Village_Halloween_Parade_%286451249051%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Greenwich_Village_Halloween_Parade_(6451249051).jpg)

The annual [New York](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") [Halloween Parade](/wiki/New_York%27s_Village_Halloween_Parade "New York's Village Halloween Parade") in [Greenwich Village](/wiki/Greenwich_Village "Greenwich Village"), [Manhattan](/wiki/Manhattan "Manhattan"), is the world's largest Halloween parade, with millions of spectators annually.

The yearly [New York's Village Halloween Parade](/wiki/New_York%27s_Village_Halloween_Parade "New York's Village Halloween Parade") was begun in 1974; it is the world's largest Halloween parade and America's only major nighttime parade, attracting more than 60,000 costumed participants, two million spectators, and a worldwide television audience.[[203]](#cite_note-NYCHalloweenParade-205)

Since the late 2010s, [ethnic stereotypes](/wiki/Ethnic_stereotype "Ethnic stereotype") as costumes have increasingly come under scrutiny in the United States.[[204]](#cite_note-206)[[205]](#cite_note-207)[[206]](#cite_note-208)

### Pet costumes

According to a 2018 report from the [National Retail Federation](/wiki/National_Retail_Federation "National Retail Federation"), 30 million Americans will spend an estimated $480 million on Halloween costumes for their pets in 2018. This is up from an estimated $200 million in 2010. The most popular costumes for pets are the pumpkin, followed by the [hot dog](/wiki/Hot_dog "Hot dog"), and the [bumblebee](/wiki/Bumblebee "Bumblebee") in third place.[[207]](#cite_note-209)

## Games and other activities

There are several games traditionally associated with Halloween. Some of these games originated as [divination](/wiki/Divination "Divination") rituals or ways of foretelling one's future, especially regarding death, marriage and children. During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), these rituals were done by a "rare few" in rural communities as they were considered to be "deadly serious" practices.[[208]](#cite_note-DiehlDonnelly2011-210) In recent centuries, these divination games have been "a common feature of the household festivities" in Ireland and Britain.[[136]](#cite_note-Hutton,_p.380-138) They often involve apples and hazelnuts. In [Celtic mythology](/wiki/Celtic_mythology "Celtic mythology"), [apples](/wiki/Apple_(symbolism) "Apple (symbolism)") were strongly associated with the [Otherworld](/wiki/Celtic_Otherworld "Celtic Otherworld") and [immortality](/wiki/Immortality "Immortality"), while [hazelnuts](/wiki/Hazel#Mythology_and_folklore "Hazel") were associated with divine wisdom.[[209]](#cite_note-211) Tamanna Nangia also suggests that they derive from Roman practices in celebration of [Pomona](/wiki/Pomona_(mythology) "Pomona (mythology)").[[72]](#cite_note-Britannica-74)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Hallowe%27en_at_Merryvale_3.jpg/250px-Hallowe%27en_at_Merryvale_3.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hallowe%27en_at_Merryvale_3.jpg)

Children [bobbing](/wiki/Apple_bobbing "Apple bobbing") for apples at Hallowe'en

The following activities were a common feature of Halloween in Ireland and Britain during the 17th–20th centuries. Some have become more widespread and continue to be popular today.
One common game is [apple bobbing](/wiki/Apple_bobbing "Apple bobbing") or dunking (which may be called "dooking" in Scotland)[[210]](#cite_note-212) in which apples float in a tub or a large basin of water and the participants must use only their teeth to remove an apple from the basin. Variants of dunking involve kneeling on a chair, holding a fork between the teeth and trying to drive the fork into an apple, or embedding a coin in the apple which participants had to remove with their teeth. Another common game involves hanging up treacle or syrup-coated [scones](/wiki/Scone "Scone") by strings; these must be eaten without using hands while they remain attached to the string, an activity that inevitably leads to a sticky face. A similar game involved hanging an apple from a string with a coin embedded; the coin had to be removed without using hands. Another once-popular game involves hanging a small wooden rod from the ceiling at head height, with a lit candle on one end and an apple hanging from the other. The rod is spun round, and everyone takes turns to try to catch the apple with their teeth.[[211]](#cite_note-213)

Several of the traditional activities from Ireland and Britain involve foretelling one's future partner or spouse. An apple would be peeled in one long strip, then the peel tossed over the shoulder. The peel is believed to land in the shape of the first letter of the future spouse's name.[[212]](#cite_note-214)[[213]](#cite_note-McNeill-215) Two hazelnuts would be roasted near a fire; one named for the person roasting them and the other for the person they desire. If the nuts jump away from the heat, it is a bad sign, but if the nuts roast quietly it foretells a good match.[[214]](#cite_note-216)[[215]](#cite_note-217) A salty oatmeal [bannock](/wiki/Bannock_(British_food) "Bannock (British food)") would be baked; the person would eat it in three bites and then go to bed in silence without anything to drink. This is said to result in a dream in which their future spouse offers them a drink to quench their thirst.[[216]](#cite_note-218) Unmarried women were told that if they sat in a darkened room and [gazed into a mirror](/wiki/Scrying "Scrying") on Halloween night, the face of their future husband would appear in the mirror.[[217]](#cite_note-hollister-219) The custom was widespread enough to be commemorated on greeting cards[[218]](#cite_note-220) from the late 19th century and early 20th century.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/Halloween-card-mirror-2.jpg/250px-Halloween-card-mirror-2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halloween-card-mirror-2.jpg)

In this 1904 Halloween greeting card, [divination](/wiki/Divination "Divination") is depicted: the young woman, looking into a mirror in a darkened room, hopes to catch a glimpse of her future husband.

Another popular Irish game was known as *púicíní* ("[blindfolds](/wiki/Blindfold "Blindfold")"); a person would be blindfolded and then would choose between several [saucers](/wiki/Saucer "Saucer"). The item in the saucer would provide a hint as to their future: a [ring](/wiki/Ring_(jewellery) "Ring (jewellery)") would mean that they would marry soon; [clay](/wiki/Clay "Clay"), that they would die soon, perhaps within the year; water, that they would [emigrate](/wiki/Irish_diaspora "Irish diaspora"); [rosary beads](/wiki/Rosary_beads "Rosary beads"), that they would take [Holy Orders](/wiki/Holy_Orders "Holy Orders") (become a nun, priest, monk, etc.); a [coin](/wiki/Coin "Coin"), that they would become rich; a [bean](/wiki/Bean "Bean"), that they would be poor.[[219]](#cite_note-221)[[220]](#cite_note-222)[[221]](#cite_note-223)[[222]](#cite_note-224) The game features prominently in the [James Joyce](/wiki/James_Joyce "James Joyce") short story "[Clay](/wiki/Clay_(short_story) "Clay (short story)")" (1914).[[223]](#cite_note-225)[[224]](#cite_note-226)[[225]](#cite_note-227)

In Ireland and Scotland, items would be hidden in food – usually a cake, [barmbrack](/wiki/Barmbrack "Barmbrack"), [cranachan](/wiki/Cranachan "Cranachan"), [champ](/wiki/Champ_(food) "Champ (food)") or [colcannon](/wiki/Colcannon "Colcannon") – and portions of it served out at random. A person's future would be foretold by the item they happened to find; for example, a ring meant marriage and a coin meant wealth.[[226]](#cite_note-228)

Up until the 19th century, the Halloween bonfires were also used for divination in parts of Scotland, Wales and Brittany. When the fire died down, a ring of stones would be laid in the ashes, one for each person. In the morning, if any stone was mislaid it was said that the person it represented would not live out the year.[[121]](#cite_note-hutton365-369-123) In Mexico, children create altars to invite the spirits of deceased children to return (*angelitos*).[[227]](#cite_note-229)

Telling [ghost stories](/wiki/Ghost_story "Ghost story"), listening to Halloween-themed songs and watching horror films are common fixtures of Halloween parties. Episodes of television series and [Halloween-themed specials](/wiki/List_of_Halloween_television_specials "List of Halloween television specials") (with the specials usually aimed at children) are commonly aired on or before Halloween, while new horror films are often released before Halloween to take advantage of the holiday.

## Haunted attractions

Main article: [Haunted attraction](/wiki/Haunted_attraction "Haunted attraction")

Haunted attractions are entertainment venues designed to thrill and scare patrons. Most attractions are seasonal Halloween businesses that may include [haunted houses](/wiki/Haunted_attraction_(simulated)#Types_of_haunted_attractions "Haunted attraction (simulated)"), [corn mazes](/wiki/Corn_maze "Corn maze"), and [hayrides](/wiki/Hayride "Hayride"),[[228]](#cite_note-hvmag-230) and the level of sophistication of the effects has risen as the industry has grown.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Ura_and_ima.jpg/250px-Ura_and_ima.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ura_and_ima.jpg)

Humorous [tombstones](/wiki/Headstone "Headstone") in front of a house in California

The first recorded purpose-built haunted attraction was the [Orton and Spooner](/wiki/Orton_and_Spooner "Orton and Spooner") Ghost House, which opened in 1915 in [Liphook](/wiki/Liphook "Liphook"), England. This attraction actually most closely resembles a carnival fun house, powered by steam.[[229]](#cite_note-231)[[230]](#cite_note-232) The House still exists, in the [Hollycombe Steam Collection](/wiki/Hollycombe_Steam_Collection "Hollycombe Steam Collection").

It was during the 1930s, about the same time as [trick-or-treating](/wiki/Trick-or-treating "Trick-or-treating"), that Halloween-themed haunted houses first began to appear in America. It was in the late 1950s that haunted houses as a major attraction began to appear, focusing first on California. Sponsored by the Children's Health Home Junior Auxiliary, the San Mateo Haunted House opened in 1957. The San Bernardino Assistance League Haunted House opened in 1958. Home haunts began appearing across the country during 1962 and 1963. In 1964, the San Manteo Haunted House opened, as well as the Children's Museum Haunted House in Indianapolis.[[231]](#cite_note-LisaMorton-233)

The haunted house as an American cultural icon can be attributed to the opening of [The Haunted Mansion](/wiki/The_Haunted_Mansion "The Haunted Mansion") in [Disneyland](/wiki/Disneyland "Disneyland") on 12 August 1969.[[232]](#cite_note-234) [Knott's Berry Farm](/wiki/Knott%27s_Berry_Farm "Knott's Berry Farm") began hosting its own Halloween night attraction, [Knott's Scary Farm](/wiki/Knott%27s_Scary_Farm "Knott's Scary Farm"), which opened in 1973.[[233]](#cite_note-235) Evangelical Christians adopted a form of these attractions by opening one of the first "hell houses" in 1972.[[234]](#cite_note-236)

The first Halloween haunted house run by a nonprofit organization was produced in 1970 by the Sycamore-Deer Park [Jaycees](/wiki/United_States_Junior_Chamber "United States Junior Chamber") in [Clifton, Ohio](/wiki/Clifton,_Cincinnati "Clifton, Cincinnati"). It was cosponsored by [WSAI](/wiki/WSAI "WSAI"), an AM radio station broadcasting out of [Cincinnati](/wiki/Cincinnati "Cincinnati"), Ohio. It was last produced in 1982.[[235]](#cite_note-237) Other Jaycees followed suit with their own versions after the success of the Ohio house. The [March of Dimes](/wiki/March_of_Dimes "March of Dimes") copyrighted a "Mini haunted house for the March of Dimes" in 1976 and began fundraising through their local chapters by conducting haunted houses soon after. Although they apparently quit supporting this type of event nationally sometime in the 1980s, some March of Dimes haunted houses have persisted until today.[[236]](#cite_note-238)

On the evening of 11 May 1984, in Jackson Township, New Jersey, the [Haunted Castle](/wiki/Haunted_Castle_(Six_Flags_Great_Adventure) "Haunted Castle (Six Flags Great Adventure)") at Six Flags Great Adventure [caught fire](/wiki/Haunted_Castle_(Six_Flags_Great_Adventure)#1984_fire "Haunted Castle (Six Flags Great Adventure)"). As a result of the fire, eight teenagers perished.[[237]](#cite_note-239) The backlash to the tragedy was a tightening of regulations relating to safety, building codes and the frequency of inspections of attractions nationwide. The smaller venues, especially the nonprofit attractions, were unable to compete financially, and the better funded commercial enterprises filled the vacuum.[[238]](#cite_note-240)[[239]](#cite_note-241) Facilities that were once able to avoid regulation because they were considered to be temporary installations now had to adhere to the stricter codes required of permanent attractions.[[240]](#cite_note-242)[[241]](#cite_note-243)[[242]](#cite_note-244)

In the late 1980s and early 1990s, theme parks became a notable figure in the Halloween business. [Six Flags Fright Fest](/wiki/Six_Flags_Fright_Fest "Six Flags Fright Fest") began in 1986 and [Universal Studios Florida](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Florida "Universal Studios Florida") began [Halloween Horror Nights](/wiki/Halloween_Horror_Nights "Halloween Horror Nights") in 1991. [Knott's Scary Farm](/wiki/Knott%27s_Scary_Farm "Knott's Scary Farm") experienced a surge in attendance in the 1990s as a result of America's obsession with Halloween as a cultural event. Theme parks have played a major role in globalizing the holiday. [Universal Studios Singapore](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Singapore "Universal Studios Singapore") and [Universal Studios Japan](/wiki/Universal_Studios_Japan "Universal Studios Japan") both participate, while Disney now mounts [Mickey's Not-So-Scary Halloween Party](/wiki/Mickey%27s_Not-So-Scary_Halloween_Party "Mickey's Not-So-Scary Halloween Party") events at its parks in Paris, Hong Kong and Tokyo, as well as in the United States.[[243]](#cite_note-245) The theme park haunts are by far the largest, both in scale and attendance.[[244]](#cite_note-246)

## Food

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/HalloweenPumpkin2.jpg/250px-HalloweenPumpkin2.jpg)](/wiki/File:HalloweenPumpkin2.jpg)

Pumpkins for sale during Halloween

On All Hallows' Eve, many Western Christian denominations encourage [abstinence from meat](/wiki/Meat-free_days "Meat-free days"), giving rise to a variety of [vegetarian](/wiki/Christian_vegetarianism "Christian vegetarianism") foods associated with this day.[[245]](#cite_note-mader-247)

Because in the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") Halloween comes in the wake of the yearly apple harvest, [candy apples](/wiki/Candy_apple "Candy apple") (known as toffee apples outside North America), [caramel apples](/wiki/Caramel_apple "Caramel apple") or taffy apples are common Halloween treats made by rolling whole apples in a sticky sugar syrup or caramel, sometimes followed by rolling them in nuts.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Tastee-Candy-Apple-Red-Caramel-wPeanuts.jpg/250px-Tastee-Candy-Apple-Red-Caramel-wPeanuts.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tastee-Candy-Apple-Red-Caramel-wPeanuts.jpg)

A [candy apple](/wiki/Candy_apple "Candy apple")

At one time, candy apples were commonly given to trick-or-treating children, but the practice rapidly waned in the wake of widespread rumors that some individuals were embedding items like pins and [razor blades in the apples](/wiki/Poisoned_candy_myths "Poisoned candy myths") in the United States.[[246]](#cite_note-rogers_r-248) While there is evidence of such incidents,[[247]](#cite_note-249) relative to the degree of reporting of such cases, actual cases involving malicious acts are extremely rare and have never resulted in serious injury. Nonetheless, many parents assumed that such heinous practices were rampant because of the mass media. At the peak of the hysteria, some hospitals offered free X-rays of children's Halloween hauls in order to find evidence of tampering. Virtually all of the few known candy poisoning incidents involved parents who poisoned their own children's candy.[[248]](#cite_note-250)

One custom that persists in modern-day Ireland is the baking (or more often nowadays, the purchase) of a [barmbrack](/wiki/Barmbrack "Barmbrack") ([Irish](/wiki/Irish_language "Irish language"): *báirín breac*), which is a light fruitcake, into which a plain ring, a coin, and other charms are placed before baking.[[249]](#cite_note-Barmbrack-251) It is considered fortunate to be the lucky one who finds it.[[249]](#cite_note-Barmbrack-251) It has also been said that those who get a ring will find their true love in the ensuing year. This is similar to the tradition of [king cake](/wiki/King_cake "King cake") at the festival of [Epiphany](/wiki/Epiphany_(holiday) "Epiphany (holiday)"). Halloween-themed foods are also produced by companies in the lead up to the night, for example [Cadbury](/wiki/Cadbury "Cadbury") releasing Goo Heads (similar to [Creme Eggs](/wiki/Cadbury_Creme_Egg "Cadbury Creme Egg")) in spooky wrapping.[[250]](#cite_note-252)
Foods such as cakes will often be decorated with Halloween colors (typically black, orange, and purple) and motifs for parties and events. Popular themes include pumpkins, spiders, and body parts.[[251]](#cite_note-Crocker_2012-253)[[252]](#cite_note-Hood_2014_p._119-254)[[253]](#cite_note-McCrum_2015-255)

List of foods associated with Halloween:

* [Barmbrack](/wiki/Barmbrack "Barmbrack") (Ireland)
* [Bonfire toffee](/wiki/Bonfire_toffee "Bonfire toffee") (Great Britain)
* [Candy apples](/wiki/Candy_apple "Candy apple")/[toffee apples](/wiki/Toffee_apple "Toffee apple") (Great Britain and Ireland)
* [Candy apples](/wiki/Candy_apple "Candy apple"), [candy corn](/wiki/Candy_corn "Candy corn"), [candy pumpkins](/wiki/Candy_pumpkin "Candy pumpkin") (North America)
* Monkey nuts ([peanuts](/wiki/Peanut "Peanut") in their shells) (Ireland and Scotland)
* [Caramel apples](/wiki/Caramel_apple "Caramel apple")
* [Caramel corn](/wiki/Caramel_corn "Caramel corn")
* [Colcannon](/wiki/Colcannon "Colcannon") (Ireland; see [below](#Christian_observances))
* [Sweets](/wiki/Candy "Candy")/[candy](/wiki/Candy "Candy")/[chocolate](/wiki/Chocolate "Chocolate"), often with novelty shapes like skulls, [pumpkins](/wiki/Candy_pumpkin "Candy pumpkin"), bats, etc.
* Roasted [pumpkin seeds](/wiki/Pumpkin_seed "Pumpkin seed")
* Roasted [sweet corn](/wiki/Sweet_corn "Sweet corn")
* [Soul cakes](/wiki/Soul_cake "Soul cake")
* [Pumpkin pie](/wiki/Pumpkin_pie "Pumpkin pie")

## Christian observances

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Vigil_of_All_Hallows%2C_St._George%27s_Episcopal_Church_%282010%29.jpg/250px-Vigil_of_All_Hallows%2C_St._George%27s_Episcopal_Church_%282010%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Vigil_of_All_Hallows,_St._George%27s_Episcopal_Church_(2010).jpg)

The Vigil of All Hallows is being celebrated at an [Episcopal](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism") Christian church on Hallowe'en.

On Hallowe'en (All Hallows' Eve), in Poland, believers were once taught to [pray](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer") out loud as they walk through the forests in order that the souls of the dead might find comfort; in Spain, Christian priests in tiny villages toll their [church bells](/wiki/Church_bell "Church bell") in order to remind their congregants to remember the dead on All Hallows' Eve.[[254]](#cite_note-256) In Ireland, and among immigrants in Canada, a custom includes the Christian practice of [abstinence](/wiki/Fasting_and_abstinence_in_the_Catholic_Church "Fasting and abstinence in the Catholic Church"), keeping All Hallows' Eve as a [meat-free day](/wiki/Meat-free_day "Meat-free day") and serving pancakes or [colcannon](/wiki/Colcannon "Colcannon") instead.[[255]](#cite_note-257)

The [Christian Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church") traditionally observed Hallowe'en through a [vigil](/wiki/Vigil "Vigil"). Worshippers prepared themselves for feasting on the following [All Saints' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day") with prayers and fasting.[[256]](#cite_note-BBC3-258) This [church service](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") is known as the *Vigil of All Hallows* or the *Vigil of All Saints*;[[257]](#cite_note-Harvey-259)[[258]](#cite_note-CNA-260) an initiative known as *Night of Light* seeks to further spread the *Vigil of All Hallows* throughout [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom").[[259]](#cite_note-261)[[260]](#cite_note-262) After the service, "suitable festivities and entertainments" often follow, as well as a visit to the graveyard or cemetery, where flowers and candles are often placed in preparation for [All Hallows' Day](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day").[[261]](#cite_note-Armentrout-263)[[262]](#cite_note-Infeld-264) In England, Light Parties are organized by churches after worship services on Halloween with the focus on Jesus as the [Light of the World](/wiki/Light_of_the_World "Light of the World").[[263]](#cite_note-265) In Finland, because so many people visit the cemeteries on All Hallows' Eve to light [votive candles](/wiki/Votive_candle "Votive candle") there, they "are known as *valomeri*, or seas of light".[[264]](#cite_note-266)

Today, Christian attitudes towards Halloween are diverse. In the [Anglican Church](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), some [dioceses](/wiki/Diocese "Diocese") have chosen to emphasize the Christian traditions associated with All Hallows' Eve.[[265]](#cite_note-267)[[266]](#cite_note-newadvent.org-268) Some of these [practices](/wiki/Christian_worship "Christian worship") include [praying](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer"), [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting") and attending [worship services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service").[[1]](#cite_note-Fasting-1)[[4]](#cite_note-Service-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Prayer-5)

> O LORD our God, increase, we pray thee, and multiply upon us the gifts of thy grace: that we, who do prevent the glorious festival of all thy Saints, may of thee be enabled joyfully to follow them in all virtuous and godly living. Through Jesus Christ, Our Lord, who liveth and reigneth with thee, in the unity of the Holy Ghost, ever one God, world without end. Amen. —[Collect](/wiki/Collect "Collect") of the Vigil of All Saints, *[The Anglican Breviary](/wiki/The_Anglican_Breviary "The Anglican Breviary")*[[267]](#cite_note-Church1955-269)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/90/Halloween_Scripture_Candy.jpg/250px-Halloween_Scripture_Candy.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halloween_Scripture_Candy.jpg)

Halloween Scripture Candy with [gospel tract](/wiki/Tract_(literature) "Tract (literature)")

Other [Protestant Christians](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") also celebrate All Hallows' Eve as [Reformation Day](/wiki/Reformation_Day "Reformation Day"), a day to remember the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Reformation "Reformation"), alongside All Hallows' Eve or independently from it.[[268]](#cite_note-RefDay-270) This is because [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") is said to have nailed his *[Ninety-five Theses](/wiki/Ninety-five_Theses "Ninety-five Theses")* to [All Saints' Church in Wittenberg](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Church,_Wittenberg "All Saints' Church, Wittenberg") on All Hallows' Eve.[[269]](#cite_note-271) Often, "Harvest Festivals", "Hallelujah Night" or "Reformation Festivals" are held on All Hallows' Eve, in which children dress up as [Bible characters](/wiki/List_of_minor_biblical_figures "List of minor biblical figures") or [Reformers](/wiki/Protestant_Reformers "Protestant Reformers").[[270]](#cite_note-Reformers-272) In addition to distributing candy to children who are trick-or-treating on Hallowe'en, many Christians also provide [gospel tracts](/wiki/Tract_(literature) "Tract (literature)") to them. One organization, the [American Tract Society](/wiki/American_Tract_Society "American Tract Society"), stated that around 3 million gospel tracts are ordered from them alone for Hallowe'en celebrations.[[271]](#cite_note-273) Others order Halloween-themed *Scripture Candy* to pass out to children on this day.[[272]](#cite_note-Woods2013-274)[[273]](#cite_note-275)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Halloween_Costumes.jpg/250px-Halloween_Costumes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Halloween_Costumes.jpg)

[Belizean](/wiki/Belize "Belize") children dressed up as Biblical figures and Christian saints

Some Christians feel concerned about the modern celebration of Halloween because they feel it trivializes – or celebrates – [paganism](/wiki/Paganism "Paganism"), the [occult](/wiki/Occult "Occult"), or other practices and cultural phenomena deemed incompatible with their beliefs.[[274]](#cite_note-russo-276) Father [Gabriele Amorth](/wiki/Gabriele_Amorth "Gabriele Amorth"), an [exorcist](/wiki/Exorcist "Exorcist") in Rome, has said, "if English and American children like to dress up as witches and devils on one night of the year that is not a problem. If it is just a game, there is no harm in that."[[275]](#cite_note-Brandreth-277) The [Catholic Archdiocese of Boston](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Archdiocese_of_Boston "Roman Catholic Archdiocese of Boston") has organized a "Saint Fest" on Halloween.[[276]](#cite_note-www.rcab.org-278) Similarly, many contemporary Protestant churches view Halloween as a fun event for children, holding events in their churches where children and their parents can dress up, play games, and get candy for free. To these Christians, Halloween holds no threat to the spiritual lives of children: being taught about death and mortality, and the ways of the Celtic ancestors actually being a valuable life lesson and a part of many of their parishioners' heritage.[[277]](#cite_note-CelticChristians-279) Christian minister Sam Portaro wrote that Halloween is about using "humor and ridicule to confront the power of death".[[278]](#cite_note-280)
